title: Build Yourself Flowers
body:
Build Yourself Flowers

This is an edited transcript of the keynote I gave at the Applied Machine Learning Conference in Charlottesville, VA in April 2026.

I first wrote a draft of this talk by hand. This part took 2 months. I then recorded myself giving a version of this talk with MacWhisper, and transcribed it with Whisper locally. This part took 45 minutes. Then I ran it through Gemini Flash 2.5 running in Pi to break into paragraphs. Then I went through the text paragraph by paragraph manually to make corrections, remove redundant phrasing and pauses, and added clarifications to make it more legible than a talk. This part took 3 hours.

Before all that, I generated the content for this talk. This part took 13 years.

I'm Vicki, and I build machine learning systems. I've built and broken systems at Tumblr, at Automattic, at Duo, at Mozilla.ai. Now I build realtime personalization and search systems at Malachyte.

We've had a lot of different conversations about how we should incorporate LLMs into machine learning workflows. And the question that came to me is, if all we're doing is generative AI, where does traditional machine learning even fit in here? So the larger question behind my existential angst is, what is the state of machine learning engineering as an industry today? Is it still worth doing machine learning engineering?

And the second question was, not only is it still worth doing ML, but in an era where we're having LLMs generate a lot of code, when the most important thing is for us to ship quickly, is it still worth doing machine learning well?

[short pause]

I wanted to take a step back and talk about flowers.

In 2024, the lovely folks at PyData Amsterdam invited me to give a keynote. I called it Build and Keep Your Context Window. ChatGPT was still fairly new, out for just about a year and a half at the time, and everyone was freaking out. I talked about how important it is to build and keep your own context window, the historical understanding of software tools and concepts, so that we can use the new tools and understand where they fit into the context of existing software engineering.

Before I gave this talk, I got to spend a few days in Amsterdam, and particularly in the Rijksmuseum, which is the national Dutch museum of art. It's absolutely enormous and beautiful.

One of the artists whose paintings are also on display at the Rijksmuseum is Rachel Ruysch, one of the premier flower painters during the Dutch Golden Age. Being a premier painter of the Dutch Golden Age was a huge accomplishment, and I want to understand more about how she works, because maybe we can use her as a guide to navigate this crazy time where we're doing a lot of code generation with LLMs.

[short pause]

Rachel lived in Amsterdam, and all she did was paint flowers. Lots and lots of different compositions of flowers in all sorts of shapes and sizes, starting from when she was 15, well into her 80s.

Let's take a step back and talk about the Dutch Golden Age. What made this era of creativity possible? In the late 1500s, the Dutch won independence from the Spanish government. The political stability was followed by economic stability and the rise of the Dutch empire. As Dutch life got significantly easier and more people joined the growing middle class, they wanted to buy art. Art became seen as an important aspect of the Dutch national character, and art which had previously been mostly religious in theme, became secular. People started painting scenes from real life, science, and nature.

Another thing people wanted was a lot of different paintings of flowers. Out of the five to ten million paintings that we think were created during the Dutch Golden Age, thousands of them were flowers. Why flowers? People wanted flowers in their homes that wouldn't wilt over the long Dutch winters, and that showcased how worldly and interesting the homeowners were.

And so Rachel Ruysch came into her skills at a time when people demanded this. And she was really good at it. And the question I had was, if you have all these people painting flowers, why is it important to paint good flowers? Why do you want to stand out? And the same question applies: in a world where it's easy and fast to write code, why is technical excellence still important?

As I was researching this, I came across a report from NASA. NASA has been continuously on a loop to improve quality, because it's already very dangerous to be an astronaut. They did an investigation in 2012 and came out with a report of what happens when things fail. And the one that really struck out to me was: shifting engineering excellence to insight and oversight.

There were periods when NASA outsourced a lot of their engineering to subcontractors. And the engineers who understood the design and the system design based on actual experience left. The shift that resulted eliminated independent analysis and testing and understanding of the systems. And this was what led to degradation of engineering quality. If it sounds familiar, this is because it's a very easy analogy for what happens when we outsource our work as engineers entirely to AI.

[short pause]

So, striving for technical excellence in industry saves us from engineering, product, and cost problems. Quality is important. The second reason is that software engineering is a craft, just like painting. And it takes time to get good at painting. And finally, technical excellence is important because striving for mastery is just about what it means to be human.

So if we want to paint good flowers, how do we become good at painting good flowers? What made Rachel technically excellent? The first one is that she was in an artistic family. All her great uncles were painters. Her father was a great artist and botanist. She learned discipline: how to capture minute details, gathering flowers in botanic gardens and pressing them so she could have different compositions for different seasons. During her life, her paintings sold for 3 times Rembrandt's.

Second, she painted her whole life. She spent over 60 years honing and working on her career, not even slowing down to have ten children. For her, her career was a vocation.

And finally, she remixed and experimented. She bartered for and preserved exotic flowers. She was always changing something about how she was working.

So, how can we, as engineers, be like Rachel?

I thought about all this and decided to apply these ideas to a concrete side project called Rijksearch, a semantic search engine for the Rijksmuseum's collection.

A semantic search engine searches by intent rather than keyword. You type in "chill dude" and it returns a portrait of a man with a dancing dog. Or "pensive" returns a philosopher in his study. It's a search engine that works by intent.

[short pause]

What was I thinking about remixing? First, over the past couple years, I've been thinking about embeddings a lot. In search and recommendations, vector representations of text or images matter a lot. I wrote a really long text about them called What Are Embeddings.

Second, I've been working with Redis. It's one of my favorite pieces of software because it just works. Salvatore Sanfilippo, antirez, picked up working on it again and created a new data structure called vector sets. Vector sets allow for vector similarity lookups using HNSW, a popular and efficient approximate nearest neighbor search algorithm.

Third, I've been hacking around AtProto, the protocol that powers Bluesky. A lot of the Bluesky services were written in Go.

Fourth, I worked in Python before, and was curious to explore a new language meant for high concurrency.

And of course, all the other stuff swirling around was LLMs. I'm particularly interested in the power of small, local models, and in hosted embedding APIs.

And then finally, the humanities. A lot of my projects revolve partially around the humanities because I don't think you can really have a balanced view of what developing a good product looks like if you're just looking at tech. And I like art.

All of this percolated for a year or so in my mind, and at some point, I realized I wanted to re-implement Viberary, my semantic search engine for books, but this time for the Rijksmuseum.

Building semantic search involves implementing a two tower architecture, a really common paradigm in both search and recommendations. There are generally three steps: embed the items once in batch, embed your query in realtime at inference, and compare the distance between the two.

This is a simple structure but it's very elegant because it allows for fast indexing and fast lookup on a large dataset. When we do information retrieval, our goal is to get from tens of millions of items down to tens that you can look at in a list of results. Two towers allows us to pre-compute the embedding for our entire catalog and then filter them quickly.

[short pause]

Something that you'll notice is missing is the actual source data. Where do we get this image data? Every company gets about three innovation tokens, from Dan McKinley's famous blog post. You can spend those however you want, but then the supply is fixed. Where should we spend our innovation tokens? We should spend them on what helps us reach mastery.

For Rijksearch, I used multimodal text and image data. I changed from a locally hosted embeddings model to an API, which was easier but required cost and latency considerations. I switched to Redis vector sets for indexing. And I changed from Python Flask to Go, which was probably the innovation token that took me the longest time to overcome.

For data collection, what helps us is data sense. We have been in the trenches of collecting data for a long time. We understand the pain points. As Rob Pike said, if you've chosen the right data structures and organized things well, the algorithms will almost always become self-evident.

And this is where AI helped and where it didn't. I used AI to build the front end, which turned out nice since I'm not a front-end person. LLMs helped me with Go, but they didn't help by writing all of the code. I learned Go step by step: reading books, trying out Go by Example, and asking about different Go concepts from the LLMs, or working with a very small method-level snippet at a time.

What happened when I was having the model write Go directly was that due to cognitive offloading, I couldn't understand what I'd written at all, and when I got up from the computer, I couldn't replicate it. But it helped me answer questions like how structs work, what you should put in a struct, what a channel does.

LLMs also helped with data formatting and exploration. Writing all the matplotlib methods, the pandas methods, all that glue stuff where you need to constantly look stuff up.

Where did it not help? Largely with architecture changes and decisions. Understanding that I needed a two-tower model, understanding my latency constraints, knowing that serving this app has to be fast: all of that came from my head and past experience rather than AI.

[short pause]

It takes time to build good things. It took Rachel 60 years to get good at painting. You can see one of her first paintings and then one of her much later paintings. Take a look at how much more complex the composition is, how the flowers are behind each other, how much more delicate the background is. It takes so much time to get good at that stuff. And we have to remind ourselves in the world of speed that that's true for programming too.

I found it takes time because I was going through my tweet archive. In 2013, I wrote about how I was learning Python and counting words and just really struggling through it. And then 10 years later I was able to do Viberary through deliberate practice. And three years later, I did it again in Go with multimodal embeddings.

And the other thing is, I didn't work on my craft alone. Anne Lamott talks about this alphabet radio that plays in your head. Of all the past people that have either criticized or praised you and how you have to tune some of them out selectively when you work. For me, it's all the people that have reviewed my code and that I've worked with and the ones that I respect are always echoing in my head.

Programming as an automatic vision is not yet. That only comes from doing the work over and over and over again. Building a semantic search engine really took me about a month once I buckled down. But it also took me ten years.

So let's try to build ourselves flowers. Thank you.
